headlines.title: Special Session of Texas Legislature Is Called
GOVERNOR CALLS SESSION TO PASS WAREHOUSE BILL
headlines.subtitle: Bonded System Proposed to Meet Situation. TO OPEN MONDAY Colquitt Said to Favor Plan as Outlined in Farmers' Convention Here. OLD MEMBERS ATTEND Under It Cotton Receipts Will Be Made Negotiable-Indorsed by Farmers.
headlines.fullText:
Special to The Star-Telegram, _AUSTIN, AUE. 17. Governor Col- Esitt today called a special session of the legislature to convene Monday at 10 a. m. for the purpose of passing a bonded warehouse bill to ald the cotton raisers of Texas. headlines.title: ACCUSATION THREATENED OVER LEVEE
headlines.subtitle: Chairman of Board Says He'll Try to Get Indictments. TO COMPLETE WORK Will Go Into Courts to Beat Injunction Obtained by the City.
headlines.fullText:
If city officials or other persons at any time in the future attempt to cut a map in the city levees, an effort will be made to have them indieted by the county grand jury.
This was the threat made Monday by R. G. Johnson, chairman of im- provement district No. 1 of Tarrant county
At the same time Johnson declared that if the court will lift the tempo- rary injunction secured by the city. restraining the improvement beard from building a levee, the unfinished of tworfifths of a mile will be completed "When Commissioner Blanke saw a emacqua and,eut the levee beur the waterworks, people telephoned to me and asked if they should take their guns and kill him" said Johnson. "I told them to stay within the law. But It much an attempt is made again they say that the man who does will be killed. An effort will at least be made to have the man or men who are guilty indicted. The law provides a
Engineers at Work.
We have made every effort to have has um reden A report han been submitted by engi- have engineers at work and their re- port no matter what it is, will be sub- Bet Te they are of the opinion that the levee should be completed, We will ask that the commissioners agree without going into the courts. But if they won't, we will certainly go to the courts and beat them.
The county has spent more than $300.000 for new bridges at Seventh North Main, Twelfth and East Fourth streets and Samuels avenue. headlines.title: FRENCH FLEET SINKS AUSTRIAN CRUISER; 1,000 GERMANS CAPTURED
headlines.subtitle: U. S. NOT INVOLVED IN JAP ULTIMATUM TO GERMAN EMPIRE
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press
WASHINGTON, Aug. 17 .- A resolution directing Secre- tary of State Bryan to protest against the announced intention of Japan to take over the German leased territory of Kiau Chau, China, was introduced in the house today by Representa- tive Britten of Illinois.
Representative Britten said the course of Japan "appears to be a warlike step to gain a hold on Chinese territory.'
The resolution was introduced in an informal manned without special attention being directed to it and later was re- ferred to a committee.
By Associated Press
LONDON, Aug. 17 .- A dispatch from Amsterdam to the Central News says the captain of the Dutch steamer Epsilon on his arrival at Ymuiden today reported seeing a German dreadnought in the harbor at Trondheim, which had been put out of action. Her funnels were smashed and on one side she was scarred with holes from shell fire.
No confirmation of the report has reached the official news bureau here.
The official press bureau of the war office and admiralty has issued a statement saying that French troops, in the course of a rapid advance along the valley of the Schirmeck, have taken 1,000 prisoners,
The scene of the fighting of the last few days in uppet Alsace, the official announcement continues, shows the great destructive effect of the fire of the allied artillery. Trenches abandoned by the enemy are filled with dead and wounded.
A dispatch from Paris to the Havas Agency says French' troops have occupied the town of Schirmeck, twelve kilometers (seven and one-half miles) beyond Saales, in Alsace.
They took twelve field guns, twelve caissons and eight rapid-fire guns, besides the 1,000 prisoners mentioned.
The French cavalry then pushed on to Mulbach and Lutz- hansen.
Official dispatches say Greece has received information that Turkish troops are crossing Bulgarian territory and march- ing in the direction of Greece. The Hellenie government, in view of these reports, has notified Turkey that if this news should be confirmed, corresponding military and naval meas- ures will be taken immediately by Greece.
The British official news bureau today says:
"The French fleet in the Mediterranean has made a sweep up the Adriatic sea as far as Cattaro. A small Austrian cruiser of the Aspern type of 2,363 tons, was fired on by the French vessels and sunk."

headlines.title: WILSON IS NOT WORRIED
headlines.subtitle: President Confident Japanese Will Do What They Promise, Congressman Wants Government to Protest Against Mi- kado's Threat-Resolution Goes to Committee.
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
WASHINGTON, Ang. 17 .- President Wilson declared today he had no rea- son to question the good faith of the declaration of Japan in promising to observe the Integrity of China in con- nection with the Japanese ultimatum to Germany demanding the surrender of Kiau Chau to the Japanese govern- ment. This ultimatum was delivered Sunday. The President would not dis- cuss the ultimatum as a whole, saying that one of the duties of neutrality was to have no opinion on what other nations were doilis.
White House Secretary Bryan was at the early this morning sup- poseilly anese in connection with an wap- ultimatum. Both the President and Mr. Bryan refused to discuss the Iasked in forwarding the ultimatum,
The President made it clear that the United States would not be Involved in any way in the Japan-Germany con- troversy, and spoke of the assurances Integrity of China as having been given to the powers generally, as well as the United States.
Asked particularly as to the prom- we of Japan for the "eventual resto- ration to China" of the German prov- inces, he indicated his confidence in the good faith of Japan to carry out that assurance at the close of the war.
